pages.title: Matt Rubin Appointed Chief Marketing Officer of Popeyes® U.S. & Canada
pages.content:
Press Releases
•
January 15, 2026
Matt Rubin Appointed Chief Marketing Officer of Popeyes® U.S. & Canada
MIAMI, FL
– Jan. 14, 2026 – Today, Popeyes® announced Matt Rubin as Chief Marketing Officer for the U.S. and Canada. In this role, Rubin will lead Popeyes’ marketing strategy, brand positioning, and customer engagement across all channels.
“Matt brings a deep understanding of the Popeyes brand, the guest, and the broader quick-service restaurant landscape,” said Peter Perdue, President of Popeyes U.S. and Canada. “His experience across marketing, analytics, and digital positions him well to move quickly to strengthen our brand positioning and drive growth.”
Rubin has nearly 11 years of experience with Restaurant Brands International Inc., with four of those with Popeyes U.S. & Canada. Most recently, he served as Popeyes Chief Digital Officer. Prior to that, Rubin led Marketing Analytics for Popeyes U.S. & Canada and served as Chief Marketing Officer for RBI’s brands in the Asia-Pacific region, where he delivered strong results across sales, traffic, and franchise profitability.
“I’m excited to step into this role and continue building on the strong foundation of the Popeyes brand,” said Rubin. “Popeyes has incredible equity rooted in its Louisiana heritage and industry-leading food, and I look forward to helping fuel the next phase of growth.”
About Popeyes®
Founded in New Orleans in 1972, Popeyes® has over 50 years of history and culinary tradition. Popeyes® distinguishes itself with a unique New Orleans style menu featuring fried chicken, chicken tenders, fried shrimp, and other regional items. The chain’s passion for its Louisiana heritage and flavorful authentic food has allowed Popeyes® to become one of the world’s largest chicken quick service restaurants with over 4,000 restaurants in the U.S. and around the world. To learn more about the brand, please visit the Popeyes® brand website at
www.popeyes.com
or follow us on Facebook, Twitter and Instagram.

pages.title: Popeyes Isn't Meeting its Potential, and it Starts with Operations - QSR Magazine
pages.content:
Naturally, a lot has happened since Popeyes’ August 2019 chicken sandwich splash. Through COVID, the brand found itself balancing
AUVs $400,000 higher than pre-launch
and, more recently, unveiled advertising and design changes with an
“Easy to Love” revamp that had 85 percent commitment from restaurants last February.
But the reality is, Popeyes’ surge has sloped of late. Its U.S. same-store sales declined 4.9 percent in Q4 (two-year stack of 4.8 percent) and fell 2.9 percent for the 12-month period that ended December 31 (2.3 percent over two years).
Popeyes same-store sales trends (U.S.)
Q4 2025: –4.9 percent
Q3 2025: –2 percent
Q2 2025: –0.9 percent
Q1 2025: –4 percent
Q4 2024: 0.1 percent
Q3 2024: –4 percent
Q2 2024: 0.5 percent
Q1 2024: 5.7 percent
Q4 2023: 5.5 percent
Q3 2023: 7 percent
Q2 2023: 6.3 percent
Q1 2023: 5.6 percent
Restaurant Brands International CEO Josh Kobza said 2025 reinforced a clear reality for the brand, and one that’s surfaced over the years: While the chicken category remains competitive and Popeyes’ product measures up against the field, its store-level execution can’t say the same.
“We know Popeyes is capable of much more, and we’re taking decisive action to put the brand back on the right path while supporting our franchisees to deliver stronger results at the restaurant level,” Kobza said.
One reaction was a leadership change. And unsurprisingly, RBI turned to a chief operating officer. Burger King U.S. COO Peter Perdue shifted over to Popeyes U.S. and Canada president roughly four months ago, succeeding Jeff Klein, who had taken over for Sami Siddiqui (now CFO of the company) in March 2024. RBI handed him a direct mandate, Kobza said, to raise operational consistency. “And he’s moving quickly,” he said, “resetting his leadership team and engaging with our franchisees.”
Perdue has been with RBI since 2013. He’s clocked jobs from regional president of Asia Pacific and VP, Burger King U.S. and Canada to the most recent COO title, which he stepped into in 2023. He was on the ground level of Burger King’s multi-pronged Reclaim the Flame turnround. Matt Rubin, formerly chief digital officer and an 11-year RBI vet, was named CMO in January (he also led marketing previously for RBI’s Asia-Pacific region brands). Bobby O’Brien was appointed head chef for Popeyes U.S. and Canda late 2025 as well. He’d been with the brand 17 years, working under chef Amy Alarcon, who headed to Burger King.
“At its core, the chicken business is a service business,” Kobza said, “and winning requires consistent speed, accuracy, and reliability in every restaurant, every day.”
Popeyes is working to expand field engagement and provide targeted support to its lowest-performing restaurants. Already, the chain increased its field operations team by about 75 percent. That’s included the launch of in-restaurant coaching visits and restaurant GM “experience rallies”—a first for Popeyes—which are slated for spring.
Alongside operations, Kobza said, Popeyes will sharpen its core product focus and prioritize offerings that define the brand and resonate with legacy and new customers, “including our incredible hand battered and fried bone-in chicken tenders and sandwich.” More to come at RBI’s February 26 investor day.
“In the meantime, I want to reiterate my confidence in the underlying strength of the Popeyes brand. We have a great group of engaged franchisees, a relatively modern asset base, solid unit economics and some of the best chicken in the industry,” Kobza said. “With disciplined execution and sustained focus, I’m very confident Popeyes will return to the level of performance it’s capable of delivering.”
RBI hasn’t shied from talking about Popeyes’ gaps in recent quarters. The juxtaposition of category-leading food with uneven service has been a recurring theme. Chairman Patrick Doyle in June said he’d put Popeyes menu up against any brand in the category. But he also acknowledged Chick-fil-A set an execution bar Popeyes wasn’t meeting.
The “Easy to Love” strategy, which circles these challenges, dates to 2023. The program calls for simplifying restaurant operations for franchisees and making kitchens easier to run. Popeyes wants all U.S. units to have cloud-based POS systems, digital drop charts, sticky label printers, order-ready boards, kiosks, and upgraded back-of-house equipment, like auto batter makers and improved hot holding units, by year-end 2026.
Doyle said restaurants must get faster. “I think the growth from Popeyes in the U.S.,” he said at the dbAccess Global Consumer Conference, “is … going to come from running the restaurants better, better service, more accurate service.”
Popeyes reported net restaurant growth of 1.6 percent in Q4 and the same for the year. It closed 2025 with 3,578 restaurants, up from 3,520, year-over-year. Coming into 2026, Popeyes’ U.S. unit count had grown by about 400 restaurants over a three-calendar stretch.
The brand opened a net of 97 stores in 2024 after debuting 130 in 2023 and 167 in 2022. It said it hopes to get back into the 150-per year range once operations steady.
Doyle in RBI’s Q4 earnings noted Popeyes’ step back in unit economics last year differed from
what happened at Burger King
, where the chain would have been essentially been flat in profitability if not for rising beef costs.
Popeyes, however, is less a macro victim and more self-inflicted. “We’ve been very upfront that sales are not where they should be,” Doyle said, “and you saw us make leadership changes in 2025 and earlier this year as a result. I’m confident that the steps we’re taking, particularly the renewed focus on operations, consistency, and brand standards will translate into better performance over time.”
The brand’s average profitability of roughly $235,000, he added, “is not where the system can or should be.”
“A” operators of Popeyes, Siddiqui said, are generating closer to $300,000, showing the carrot to chase.
Popeyes wants to refocus efforts on its core items.
Popeyes international has been a different story. Doyle called it a “stunningly great business.” For perspective, Popeyes in 2023 generated system sales outside the country of $927 million. Last year, it was $1.7 billion. Popeyes international generated $500 million in Q4 alone and stands at a run rate of $2 billion.
When RBI unveiled its international progress in 2025
, Popeyes was at 1,400 or so international units in 45 markets, with ample runway. It boasted about 300 stores in the Asia Pacific. Its largest competitor (KFC) has more than 19,000.
Just a few years ago, there were no Popeyes in the U.K. Now, there are 100-plus, with some of the better unit economics in the system—over $3 million top-line boxes.
Returning to the larger conversation, Kobza said the “chicken category is amazing” and a great place for Popeyes to be in, stateside and otherwise, current state or potential one. The sagging sales through recent quarters (leading to the change atop) triggered two main learnings, as noted—a need to make quicker progress on consistency of operations, and rethinking marketing and product.
Kobza said Popeyes spent more time in non-core categories over the last year, year-and-a-half than it should have. So, it’s going to recenter focus on “the things that made Popeyes great.”
It’s going to narrow messaging and reengage loyal customers of Popeyes’ bone-in chicken, tenders, and the sandwich.
The chain started increasing national advertising spend last April. That step-up was scheduled to continue for three years if performance targets were met. RBI also encouraged franchisees to remodel restaurants backed by results that A-grade units were generated 30 percent higher profitability than the system average. A longer-view goal is to reach a consistent modern image by 2030.
Still, like Burger King, Popeyes plans to become more intentional about prioritizing operational consistency over new development.
And despite a January bankruptcy to 136-unit Popeyes franchisee Sailormen
, a group with about $130 million in debt that blamed the lasting impact of COVID, changing consumer choices, rising inflation, higher borrowing rates, and a limited qualified labor pool for its struggles, Kobza said the rest of the U.S. franchisee system is “actually in a quite healthy place.”
“We acquired the business about nine years ago,” Kobza said of Popeyes (RBI acquired the chain in 2017 for $1.8 billion). “It has been a tremendous run. It’s had a little bit of some ups and downs along the way. But it’s really been great, both in the U.S. and around the world.”
“If you actually go back to when we got involved in the business in 2016, what created that opportunity was a bit of a wobble in the business at that time,” he added. “And that created an opportunity to acquire a brand in one of the most attractive, if not the most attractive, segments in the entire world. And after that point in time, we managed to produce an incredible nine years of growth. I think we’ve tripled or quadrupled that business. And I hope we’ll do that again now under Peter’s leadership.”

pages.title: Popeyes promotes Matt Rubin to chief marketing officer
pages.content:
Sponsored By
Quick Service
Marketing & Branding
News
Popeyes promotes Matt Rubin to chief marketing officer
His appointment comes as the chain experiences softer sales and aims to shift its focus back to its core products and consistent value messaging
Alicia Kelso,
Executive Editor,
Nation's Restaurant News
January 15, 2026
2 Min Read
Matt Rubin has been named CMO of Popeyes U.S. and Canada
Photo courtesy of Popeyes
Popeyes Louisiana Kitchen has named Matt Rubin chief marketing officer for the United States and Canada. He is charged with leading the quick-service chicken chain’s marketing strategy, brand positioning, and customer engagement across all channels.
“Matt brings a deep understanding of the Popeyes brand, the guest, and the broader quick-service restaurant landscape,” Popeyes president Peter Perdue said in a statement. “His experience across marketing, analytics, and digital positions him well to move quickly to strengthen our brand positioning and drive growth.”
Rubin has nearly 11 years of experience with parent company Restaurant Brands International Inc., which is also the parent company of Burger King, Tim Hortons, and Firehouse Subs. Most recently, he served as Popeyes chief digital officer. Prior, he led marketing analytics for Popeyes U.S. & Canada and served as CMO for RBI’s brands in the Asia-Pacific region, where he delivered strong results across sales, traffic, and franchise profitability, according to the company.
Related:
McDonald’s showcases employees in reality TV-inspired campaign
“I’m excited to step into this role and continue building on the strong foundation of the Popeyes brand,” Rubin said in a statement. “Popeyes has incredible equity rooted in its Louisiana heritage and industry-leading food, and I look forward to helping fuel the next phase of growth.”
Rubin’s appointment comes less than a year and a half after Popeyes named Bart LaCount to the CMO role.
Popeyes had a tough 2025, including -2% same-store sales in the most recently reported third quarter. Those results followed a 0.9% decline in same-store sales in Q2, and -4% in Q1.
During the company’s Q3 earnings call, RBI executives acknowledged the chain’s year had been “softer,” and vowed to shift its focus back to core products and consistent value messaging.
“We can shift some of our marketing and innovation focus. You've seen things like Dippers and Pickles, which gain a lot of customer interest, but sometimes don't drive the sustained sales growth that we'd like to see. So, you're probably going to see us shift back a bit of that focus to some of our more core platforms. So those are the places that I'm focused on. I think that's what's going to drive the turnaround,” RBI chief executive officer Josh Kobza told analysts. “If you go back over the last few years, we grew our same-store sales tremendously when we focused on the core and got things right.”
Contact Alicia Kelso at
Alicia.Kelso@informa.com
Follow her on TikTok: @aliciakelso
Read more about:
Popeyes
About the Author
Alicia Kelso
Executive Editor, Nation's Restaurant News
Alicia Kelso is the executive editor of Nation's Restaurant News. She began covering the restaurant industry in 2010 for QSRweb.com, FastCasual.com, and PizzaMarketplace.com. When her son was born, she left the industry to pursue a role in higher education, but swiftly returned after realizing how much she missed the space. In filling that void, Alicia added a contributor role at Restaurant Dive and a senior contributor role at Forbes.
Her work has appeared in publications around the world, including Forbes Asia, NPR, Bloomberg, The Seattle Times, Crain's Chicago, Good Morning America, and Franchise Asia Magazine.
Alicia holds a degree in journalism from Bowling Green State University, where she competed on the women's swim team. In addition to cheering for the BGSU Falcons, Alicia is a rabid Michigan fan and will talk about college football with anyone willing to engage. She lives in Louisville, Kentucky, with her wife and son.
Follow her on TikTok
@aliciakelso
